Data source

This report uses anonymised/synthetic data (simulated from structure). No real observations are shown.

Experiment and Data

The analysis uses the synthetic data dataset. The outcome is v1, with v4 (exposure), v3 (mediator), and v2 (covariate) as predictors. After cleaning and keeping complete cases on the outcome and covariate, the data were used to model v1 as a function of v3, v4, v2, and the v3–v4 interaction.

Methods

A Bayesian regression model was fitted in R (R Core Team 2022) using the brms package (Bürkner, 2017) with the cmdstanr backend. The model was a structural equation model with two equations: (1) v3 is regressed on v4 (exposure affects mediator); (2) log(v1) (outcome log-transformed) is regressed on v3, v4, their interaction, and v2, with a Gaussian distribution. So v4 affects the outcome both directly and indirectly via v3, and v2 is an additional predictor (v3 ~ v4; log(v1) ~ v3 * v4 + v2). The model was fitted with 4 chains, 1000 warmup and 2000 iterations per chain. Model validation relied on posterior predictive checks (density overlay) and standard MCMC diagnostics (Rhat and effective sample size) (McElreath, 2020).

Why Bayesian?
  • This framework is particularly useful for the analysis of complex data sets with temporal and spatial (hierarchical) structures and is highly flexible to model the data generating process: https://doi.org/10.1007/s10144-015-0503-4

Analysis

#> tibble [153 × 8] (S3: tbl_df/tbl/data.frame)
#>  $ v1    : num [1:153] 117.3 27.8 43.1 13.5 105.5 ...
#>  $ v2    : num [1:153] 310.2 102.1 77.6 134.3 300.8 ...
#>  $ v3    : num [1:153] 11.9 2.93 15.29 14.23 12.34 ...
#>  $ v4    : num [1:153] 72 75.6 78.1 73.8 63.4 ...
#>  $ v5    : chr [1:153] "L2" "L5" "L1" "L3" ...
#>  $ v6    : num [1:153] 12.68 27.79 24.24 8.75 25.49 ...
#>  $ v7    : num [1:153] 0.272 0.252 0.979 0.263 0.621 ...
#>  $ log_v1: num [1:153] 4.76 3.33 3.76 2.6 4.66 ...
#>  Family: MV(gaussian, gaussian) 
#>   Links: mu = identity
#>          mu = identity 
#> Formula: v3 ~ v4 
#>          log_v1 ~ v3 * v4 + v2 
#>    Data: data (Number of observations: 153) 
#>   Draws: 4 chains, each with iter = 2000; warmup = 1000; thin = 1;
#>          total post-warmup draws = 4000
#> 
#> Regression Coefficients:
#>                 Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS
#> v3_Intercept       12.03      2.99     6.21    17.92 1.00     3605     3084
#> logv1_Intercept     3.47      0.92     1.65     5.30 1.00     2273     2279
#> v3_v4              -0.01      0.04    -0.09     0.07 1.00     3649     3182
#> logv1_v3            0.05      0.08    -0.10     0.20 1.00     2103     2263
#> logv1_v4            0.01      0.01    -0.01     0.04 1.00     2230     2388
#> logv1_v2            0.00      0.00    -0.00     0.00 1.00     4499     2618
#> logv1_v3:v4        -0.00      0.00    -0.00     0.00 1.00     2088     2168
#> 
#> Further Distributional Parameters:
#>             Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS
#> sigma_v3        5.60      0.32     5.01     6.27 1.00     3359     2263
#> sigma_logv1     0.81      0.05     0.72     0.91 1.00     3127     2583
#> 
#> Draws were sampled using sample(hmc). For each parameter, Bulk_ESS
#> and Tail_ESS are effective sample size measures, and Rhat is the potential
#> scale reduction factor on split chains (at convergence, Rhat = 1).

Stancode

#> // generated with brms 2.23.0
#> functions {
#> }
#> data {
#>   int<lower=1> N;  // total number of observations
#>   int<lower=1> N_v3;  // number of observations
#>   vector[N_v3] Y_v3;  // response variable
#>   int<lower=1> K_v3;  // number of population-level effects
#>   matrix[N_v3, K_v3] X_v3;  // population-level design matrix
#>   int<lower=1> Kc_v3;  // number of population-level effects after centering
#>   int<lower=1> N_logv1;  // number of observations
#>   vector[N_logv1] Y_logv1;  // response variable
#>   int<lower=1> K_logv1;  // number of population-level effects
#>   matrix[N_logv1, K_logv1] X_logv1;  // population-level design matrix
#>   int<lower=1> Kc_logv1;  // number of population-level effects after centering
#>   int prior_only;  // should the likelihood be ignored?
#> }
#> transformed data {
#>   matrix[N_v3, Kc_v3] Xc_v3;  // centered version of X_v3 without an intercept
#>   vector[Kc_v3] means_X_v3;  // column means of X_v3 before centering
#>   matrix[N_logv1, Kc_logv1] Xc_logv1;  // centered version of X_logv1 without an intercept
#>   vector[Kc_logv1] means_X_logv1;  // column means of X_logv1 before centering
#>   for (i in 2:K_v3) {
#>     means_X_v3[i - 1] = mean(X_v3[, i]);
#>     Xc_v3[, i - 1] = X_v3[, i] - means_X_v3[i - 1];
#>   }
#>   for (i in 2:K_logv1) {
#>     means_X_logv1[i - 1] = mean(X_logv1[, i]);
#>     Xc_logv1[, i - 1] = X_logv1[, i] - means_X_logv1[i - 1];
#>   }
#> }
#> parameters {
#>   vector[Kc_v3] b_v3;  // regression coefficients
#>   real Intercept_v3;  // temporary intercept for centered predictors
#>   real<lower=0> sigma_v3;  // dispersion parameter
#>   vector[Kc_logv1] b_logv1;  // regression coefficients
#>   real Intercept_logv1;  // temporary intercept for centered predictors
#>   real<lower=0> sigma_logv1;  // dispersion parameter
#> }
#> transformed parameters {
#>   // prior contributions to the log posterior
#>   real lprior = 0;
#>   lprior += student_t_lpdf(Intercept_v3 | 3, 11.4, 7.2);
#>   lprior += student_t_lpdf(sigma_v3 | 3, 0, 7.2)
#>     - 1 * student_t_lccdf(0 | 3, 0, 7.2);
#>   lprior += student_t_lpdf(Intercept_logv1 | 3, 4.6, 2.5);
#>   lprior += student_t_lpdf(sigma_logv1 | 3, 0, 2.5)
#>     - 1 * student_t_lccdf(0 | 3, 0, 2.5);
#> }
#> model {
#>   // likelihood including constants
#>   if (!prior_only) {
#>     target += normal_id_glm_lpdf(Y_v3 | Xc_v3, Intercept_v3, b_v3, sigma_v3);
#>     target += normal_id_glm_lpdf(Y_logv1 | Xc_logv1, Intercept_logv1, b_logv1, sigma_logv1);
#>   }
#>   // priors including constants
#>   target += lprior;
#> }
#> generated quantities {
#>   // actual population-level intercept
#>   real b_v3_Intercept = Intercept_v3 - dot_product(means_X_v3, b_v3);
#>   // actual population-level intercept
#>   real b_logv1_Intercept = Intercept_logv1 - dot_product(means_X_logv1, b_logv1);
#> }
test Passed
Data: no NA in v1 ↑
Data: required columns (v1, v2, v3, v4, log_v1) ↑
Data: n > 0 ↑
Data: v1 positive ↑
Data: log_v1 is numeric ↑
Model: converged (Rhat < 1.05) ↑
Model: has v3 and v4 terms ↑
Model: has v2 term ↑
Model: bivariate (2 responses) ↑

Causal diagram

i

  • A causal diagram (DAG) is a flowchart that maps out the causal relationships between the exposure (X) and outcome (Y), along with additional variables that may affect the X and Y association.

  • As such a causal diagram is a graphical representation of the hypothesis and summary of a modeling approach.

  • A biologist’s guide to model selection and causal inference: https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2020.2815

Figure 1: Causal diagram (DAG): exposure (v4), mediator (v3), covariate (v2), and outcome (log(v1)).

Raw plots

Figure 2: Observed v1 (points) and posterior predictions from the Gaussian model for log(v1), back-transformed to v1 scale. Left: v1 vs v3. Centre: v1 vs v4. Right: v1 vs v2. Shaded bands show the 95% credible interval; the solid line is the posterior median.

Effect plots

Figure 3: Interaction effect of v3 on v1 at low vs high v4 (25th and 75th percentiles). Grey shading is the 95% credible interval; the black line is the posterior median. Legend indicates v4 level.

Figure 4: Effect of v3 on v1 at low vs high v2 (25th and 75th percentiles). Grey shading is the 95% credible interval; the black line is the posterior median. Legend indicates v2 level.

Results

The causal diagram (Figure 1) encodes the assumption that v3, v4, and v2 influence v1. The raw plots (Figure 2, 2-column layout) show that observed v1 tends to decrease with higher v3 and to increase with higher v4 and v2, with the model capturing these patterns and uncertainty. The interaction effect plot (Figure 3) shows how the effect of v3 on v1 differs at low vs high v4; Figure 4 shows the effect of v3 at low vs high v2. Non-parallel curves indicate an interaction. In this Bayesian framework, a predictor is typically considered to have a meaningful effect when its 95% credible interval excludes zero.

References

Bürkner, P.C., (2017). brms: An R Package for Bayesian Multilevel Models Using Stan. Journal of Statistical Software, 80(1), 1-28. https://econpapers.repec.org/article/jssjstsof/v_3a080_3ai01.htm

McElreath, R. (2020). Statistical rethinking: A Bayesian course with examples in R and Stan. Chapman and Hall/CRC.

R Core Team (2022). R: A language and environment for statistical computing. R Foundation for Statistical Computing, Vienna, Austria. https://www.R-project.org/
